summary: Jeff Atwood explains the technical significance of high dynamic range (HDR) lighting in video games, covering the nVidia 7800 launch event. He describes how traditional 32-bit color limits brightness to only 256 levels, vastly underrepresenting both real-world light (10^12 to 1 range) and human eye capability (1000 to 1). By moving to 64-bit values with 16 bits each for color and brightness, HDR enables dramatically more realistic rendering. Atwood quotes nVidia's chief scientist who calls HDR 'the single most significant change in visual quality' since shading, and compares the improvement to the jump from dial-up to broadband.
key_points: Traditional 32-bit color offers only 256 brightness levels, far below the human eye's 1000:1 dynamic range or reality's 10^12:1 range
HDR uses 64-bit values to provide 16 bits each for color precision and brightness intensity
LCD displays of the era had only 5-6 bits of brightness resolution, making HDR benefits invisible on them
The GeForce 7 was the first generation capable of delivering HDR without major performance penalties
Games without HDR look 'flat and featureless' with no highs and no shadow detail
money_quotes: Those 8 bits per color also represent intensity. That means the brightest white is 255, 255, 255 – only 256 times brighter than the blackest black.
I think that High Dynamic Range Lighting is going to be the single most significant change in the visual quality over the next couple of years. It's almost as big as shading.
Games without HDR look flat. They should, since they are only using a range of 256:1 in brightness – a small fraction of what our eyes can see.
It's like going from dial-up to broadband; you don't really know what you've been missing until you've seen it in action.
TANSTAAFL: increasing the data payload from 32-bit to 64-bit hurts performance.
key_insight: HDR lighting represents a fundamental shift in visual quality because traditional color depth drastically underrepresents the brightness range that both reality and human eyes can handle.
